heroOverview: African trypanosomiasis, also called sleeping sickness, is a parasitic disease caused by protozoa of the species Trypanosoma brucei, transmitted exclusively through the bite of infected tsetse flies in sub-Saharan Africa. Two subspecies drive human disease: T. b. gambiense, responsible for more than 95% of cases and producing a slow-progressing illness over months to years, and T. b. rhodesiense, causing a rapidly fatal form over weeks. The disease passes through two stages: a haemolymphatic phase affecting blood and lymph nodes, and a later meningo-encephalitic phase in which the parasite crosses the blood-brain barrier, causing the severe sleep disruption that gives the disease its name. Thanks to sustained WHO-coordinated control efforts, reported cases have fallen from roughly 37,000 in 1998 to fewer than 1,000 per year by 2021 — the lowest level in recorded history — with elimination as a public health problem targeted by 2030.

keyStats.prevalence: Fewer than 1,000 new cases reported globally per year as of 2021-2023 (WHO); disease risk concentrated in rural areas of 36 sub-Saharan African countries
keyStats.demographics: Primarily affects rural populations — farmers, fishermen, hunters — in endemic zones; children and adults equally susceptible; no age-sex predisposition to infection
keyStats.avgAge: All ages affected; historically greatest burden in working-age adults due to occupational exposure to tsetse habitat
keyStats.globalCases: At peak in the late 1990s, an estimated 300,000 people were infected annually; ongoing control has reduced this by more than 99%

definition: African human trypanosomiasis (HAT; ICD-10: B56) is a vector-borne parasitic disease caused by flagellate protozoa of the genus Trypanosoma, subspecies T. brucei gambiense (chronic, West and Central Africa; ICD-10: B56.0) and T. brucei rhodesiense (acute, East Africa; ICD-10: B56.1). Transmission occurs through the bite of infected Glossina species (tsetse flies), which deposit metacyclic trypomastigotes into the skin. The parasite evades host immunity through antigenic variation of its variant surface glycoprotein (VSG) coat, allowing sustained bloodstream infection. HAT unfolds in two distinct clinical stages. Stage 1 (haemolymphatic) involves parasite proliferation in blood, lymph, and tissues. Stage 2 (meningo-encephalitic) occurs when trypanosomes cross the blood-brain barrier and invade the central nervous system, causing the characteristic neuropsychiatric syndrome with disrupted sleep-wake cycles. The condition falls under tropical medicine and, in the CNS stage, neurology. A third related but distinct condition, animal African trypanosomiasis (nagana), affects livestock and has major economic impact in endemic regions. Historically affecting hundreds of thousands, coordinated WHO control programmes using active population screening and improved case management have reduced annual reported cases to under 1,000 as of 2021.

typesClassification.description: Caused by Trypanosoma brucei gambiense, found across West and Central Africa. Accounts for over 95% of all reported human cases. Disease progression is slow — often asymptomatic or pauci-symptomatic for months to years before CNS involvement — making it difficult to detect at early stage.

Caused by Trypanosoma brucei rhodesiense, found in East and Southern Africa. A much more rapid and virulent disease that progresses to CNS stage within weeks and is often fatal within months if untreated. Cases are fewer in number but carry higher individual case fatality.
Trypanosomes confined to blood, lymph, and peripheral tissues. Fever, lymphadenopathy, skin changes, and systemic symptoms predominate. Treatment is highly effective at this stage with low toxicity.

Trypanosomes have crossed the blood-brain barrier. Neuropsychiatric symptoms — sleep disruption, confusion, behavioural change — dominate. Treatment requires drugs that penetrate the CNS and was historically far more toxic; newer oral drug fexinidazole has transformed stage-2 management.

primarySymptoms: Trypanosomal chancre: a painful, indurated skin lesion developing at the tsetse bite site within days of infection, more commonly seen with T. b. rhodesiense than gambiense infection.
Intermittent fever with irregular episodes that fail to follow the regular periodicity of malaria, accompanied by headache and generalised malaise, often the first systemic symptom in stage 1.
Cervical lymphadenopathy — classically posterior triangle lymph node enlargement known as Winterbottom's sign — present in up to 75% of T. b. gambiense cases, a key clinical indicator.

Severe daytime sleepiness with an inverted sleep-wake cycle: patients are excessively sleepy during the day and awake at night, caused by trypanosome disruption of circadian rhythm regulation in the CNS during stage 2.
Generalised headache that is persistent, progressive, and poorly responsive to standard analgesics, reflecting CNS inflammation as the disease advances to stage 2.
Progressive neuropsychiatric changes including confusion, disorientation, personality change, agitation, and mood disturbance, occurring as trypanosomes invade brain parenchyma.

Motor disturbances in advanced CNS disease: tremor, abnormal gait, difficulty speaking (dysarthria), muscle weakness, and uncoordinated movements reflecting involvement of the basal ganglia and cerebellum.
Wasting and progressive weight loss throughout the course of illness, particularly pronounced in the chronic gambiense form during months of untreated infection.
Oedema, typically periorbital or facial, occurring in some patients with rhodesiense form and reflecting systemic inflammation and possible cardiac involvement.
Coma in the terminal stage of untreated stage-2 disease, which is uniformly fatal without treatment — the origin of the common name sleeping sickness.
earlyWarningSigns: An unexplained firm, red skin swelling at the site of a recent tsetse fly bite in a person who has visited sub-Saharan Africa.
Recurrent fever episodes not responding to standard antimalarial treatment in a visitor to or resident of an endemic area.
Posterior cervical lymph node swelling in anyone with recent exposure to tsetse fly habitat.
Unexplained progressive daytime sleepiness or nighttime wakefulness in someone from or travelling through tsetse-endemic regions.
emergencySigns: Sudden deterioration of consciousness, stupor, or coma in a patient with known or suspected trypanosomiasis, indicating advanced CNS stage requiring immediate treatment.

Severe post-treatment reactive encephalopathy (PTRE) after melarsoprol administration — characterised by convulsions, coma, and rapid neurological deterioration — a potentially fatal drug reaction occurring in up to 10% of melarsoprol recipients.
Status epilepticus developing during stage-2 disease, reflecting severe meningoencephalitic inflammation.
Signs of cardiac involvement in rhodesiense infection: new arrhythmia, heart failure, or pericarditis alongside fever in a traveller returning from East Africa.

causes.cause: Trypanosoma brucei gambiense transmission via tsetse fly
causes.description: When an infected Glossina tsetse fly bites a human, it injects metacyclic trypomastigotes into the dermis. The parasite proliferates locally, enters lymphatics and then the bloodstream, and systematically evades immune destruction by continuously switching its variant surface glycoprotein coat — the defining feature of T. brucei biology that makes natural immunity impossible.

causes.cause: Trypanosoma brucei rhodesiense transmission via tsetse fly
causes.description: The same tsetse-mediated transmission mechanism applies, but T. b. rhodesiense carries an additional gene (SRA, serum resistance-associated) that confers resistance to human innate immunity via apolipoprotein L-I — the primary factor behind its greater human virulence and faster disease progression compared with gambiense.

Tsetse flies breed in dense riverine vegetation, woodland, and forest in sub-Saharan Africa. Occupational or recreational activities bringing humans into these habitats — farming along river banks, fishing, wildlife tourism in game parks — provide the necessary exposure. The flies bite during daylight hours and are attracted to movement and dark-coloured clothing.

T. b. rhodesiense is a zoonosis; cattle, wild game (bushbuck, hartebeest), and other mammals serve as natural reservoirs. Human infection arises incidentally when people enter habitats shared with infected animals and tsetse. This reservoir makes eradication of rhodesiense much harder than gambiense, which is primarily a human-to-human cycle.

causes.cause: Mother-to-child transmission and rare blood-borne routes
causes.description: Congenital trypanosomiasis via transplacental transmission has been documented in gambiense infection and can cause miscarriage, stillbirth, or neonatal disease. Transmission via blood transfusion and needle sharing has also been reported, though these routes are quantitatively minor compared with vector-borne transmission.

riskFactors.factor: Residence or prolonged travel in tsetse-endemic rural Africa
riskFactors.description: The 36 countries in sub-Saharan Africa with active tsetse populations encompass an estimated 55 million people at risk; rural dwellers with daily contact with woodland or riverine vegetation are at greatest risk because urban areas lack tsetse habitat.

riskFactors.factor: Occupational exposure — farming, fishing, wildlife work
riskFactors.description: Occupations requiring entry into tsetse habitat during daylight hours — subsistence farming along river banks, artisanal fishing, hunting, and game park employment — account for the majority of HAT cases; protective clothing and repellent use can reduce this risk.

riskFactors.factor: Wildlife tourism in East African game parks (rhodesiense risk)
riskFactors.description: Travellers on safari in national parks of Uganda, Tanzania, Zambia, and Zimbabwe encounter T. b. rhodesiense in tsetse-infested game habitat; imported rhodesiense cases from tourists are periodically reported in North America and Europe.

In areas where mobile HAT screening programmes have been suspended or never implemented, infected individuals remain undetected and act as unintentional reservoirs; the gambiense form's long asymptomatic incubation means community transmission continues silently.

In many endemic areas, agricultural and fishing work is predominantly done by men, leading to higher case rates in males; this is a proxy for occupational exposure rather than any biological susceptibility.

Civil conflict, population displacement, and healthcare system collapse in the DRC and other endemic countries led to resurgence of HAT in the 1990s-2000s; political instability remains a risk factor for regional epidemics.

Although HAT occurs in immunocompetent individuals, HIV co-infection and other causes of immunosuppression may accelerate disease progression and complicate diagnosis by altering the serological response; co-infection also complicates treatment tolerance.

affectedDemographics: Rural subsistence farmers and fishermen in the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), which accounts for over 75% of global gambiense cases
Residents of rural endemic areas in Angola, Central African Republic, South Sudan, Chad, and Guinea
Wildlife tourists and game park workers in East African countries with active rhodesiense transmission
Children and adults equally at epidemiological risk; children may be at higher risk of severe CNS outcomes due to incomplete blood-brain barrier development
Women of reproductive age in endemic regions, among whom congenital transmission is a particular concern
Travellers and humanitarian workers stationed in remote endemic areas without access to vector control measures
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of HAT requires demonstration of trypanosomes because clinical features overlap substantially with malaria, typhoid fever, viral encephalitis, and many other febrile illnesses endemic to the same regions. The diagnostic pathway differs by stage and subspecies. For T. b. gambiense, the Card Agglutination Test for Trypanosomiasis (CATT) is the standard first-line field screening tool — a rapid, inexpensive card test on whole blood or serum with sensitivity around 87-98%; a positive result then requires parasitological confirmation. The RDT (Rapid Diagnostic Test) SD BIOLINE HAT has now largely replaced CATT in national programmes. Parasitological confirmation uses microscopic examination of blood (wet film, Giemsa-stained thick film), lymph node aspirate from enlarged nodes, or concentration techniques such as the mHCT (mini anion exchange centrifugation technique) and CTC (Capillary Tube Centrifugation), which raise sensitivity to around 97% for stage-1 gambiense. The m-AECT is the WHO-recommended concentration method. For T. b. rhodesiense, there is no validated serological screening test, so diagnosis relies entirely on microscopy and is often made during evaluation of acute febrile illness in a traveller. Staging requires cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis obtained by lumbar puncture: stage 2 is defined by detection of trypanosomes in CSF, a white cell count above 5 cells/μL, or elevated intrathecal IgM. PCR-based assays are available in reference laboratories and improve sensitivity for low-parasitaemia gambiense cases. Distinguishing stage 1 from stage 2 is clinically critical because it dictates treatment choice and prognosis.

diagnosticTests.test: Rapid diagnostic test (RDT) / Card Agglutination Test for Trypanosomiasis (CATT)
diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line serological screening for T. b. gambiense in field conditions; detects anti-trypanosome antibodies in whole blood within minutes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A finger-prick blood sample is placed on the RDT card; a coloured line appears within 15-20 minutes if positive. A positive result requires confirmatory microscopy; the test does not diagnose T. b. rhodesiense.

diagnosticTests.test: Microscopy — blood film and lymph node aspirate
diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive parasitological confirmation by visualising trypanosomes directly; the only method that proves active infection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood is taken from a finger prick or vein and examined under a microscope, sometimes after concentration. If posterior cervical nodes are enlarged, a fine needle aspirate of the node is smeared, stained, and examined. Results are available same day in equipped facilities.

diagnosticTests.test: mAECT / mini anion exchange centrifugation technique
diagnosticTests.purpose: Concentration technique that raises blood microscopy sensitivity to approximately 97% for stage-1 T. b. gambiense by selectively binding trypanosomes to a resin column.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small blood sample is passed through a resin column in the laboratory; eluate is examined by microscopy. The procedure takes 30-60 minutes and is performed in a reference laboratory.

diagnosticTests.test: Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis via lumbar puncture
diagnosticTests.purpose: Determines disease stage by detecting trypanosomes or elevated white cells in CSF; a CSF WCC above 5/μL or trypanosome presence defines stage 2 and changes treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie on your side or sit hunched forward; a clinician inserts a thin needle into the lower back under local anaesthetic to collect a small amount of spinal fluid. The procedure takes 15-30 minutes; mild headache afterwards is common.

diagnosticTests.test: PCR (polymerase chain reaction) for T. brucei DNA
diagnosticTests.purpose: High-sensitivity molecular confirmation for low-parasitaemia cases, particularly gambiense in late infection when parasite numbers in blood are low; used in reference laboratories.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood or CSF sample is sent to a specialist laboratory; results take 24-48 hours. PCR is not routinely available in field settings but is essential for ambiguous cases.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count, liver function tests, and kidney function
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses systemic disease burden, anaemia, and organ dysfunction to guide treatment choice and monitoring, particularly before administering drugs with haematological and hepatic toxicity.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results in hours at a district hospital.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of HAT is stage-specific and must be overseen by clinicians experienced in tropical medicine, as therapeutic options have historically been limited by toxicity. For gambiense HAT, the WHO-recommended treatment landscape was transformed by the approval of oral fexinidazole in 2018 (EMA) and 2021 (FDA), which is now the first-line treatment for both stage 1 and non-severe stage-2 gambiense disease. Fexinidazole is an oral nitroimidazole taken once daily for 10 days that achieves success rates above 90% across stages, replacing the previously required intravenous regimens for most patients. Where fexinidazole is contraindicated — in children under 6 or under 20 kg, in pregnant women, or in very severe stage-2 disease — the oral combination acoziborole (a single oral dose) is pending regulatory approval and has shown >95% efficacy in pivotal trials; until approval, NECT (Nifurtimox-Eflornithine Combination Therapy) remains standard for non-fexinidazole-eligible stage-2 gambiense cases. For rhodesiense HAT, which carries no validated serological test and progresses rapidly, stage-1 disease is treated with intravenous suramin for seven days; stage-2 disease with melarsoprol, an arsenical compound that remains the only drug consistently active against CNS-stage rhodesiense but carries significant neurotoxicity — post-treatment reactive encephalopathy occurs in up to 10% of recipients, with 1-5% fatality. All confirmed cases must be registered with national HAT programmes and the WHO for surveillance. Follow-up CSF examination is required at 6, 12, and 24 months to confirm cure.

medicalTreatments.name: Fexinidazole (oral)
medicalTreatments.description: A nitroimidazole taken orally once daily for 10 days with food; first approved in 2018 for both stage 1 and non-severe stage-2 gambiense HAT. It is active against both blood and CNS trypanosomes. Contraindicated in pregnancy, children under 6 years or under 20 kg, severe stage-2 disease, and patients who cannot reliably take oral medication with food. Common side effects include nausea and vomiting.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical success rates of 91-98% for stage-1 and non-severe stage-2 gambiense HAT at 18-month follow-up in pivotal trials (phase II/III DNDi trials published in The Lancet, 2018-2019).

medicalTreatments.name: NECT — Nifurtimox-Eflornithine Combination Therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Intravenous eflornithine combined with oral nifurtimox; the standard treatment for stage-2 gambiense HAT in patients where fexinidazole is contraindicated (pregnancy, children, severe CNS disease). Eflornithine is given IV over two hours, four times daily for seven days; nifurtimox is given orally three times daily for ten days. The combination was introduced by WHO in 2009 as a less toxic alternative to melarsoprol for gambiense stage 2.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates of approximately 94% at 18-month follow-up in the pivotal randomised trial; case fatality below 1%, substantially lower than melarsoprol monotherapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Suramin (intravenous)
medicalTreatments.description: A polysulfonated naphthalene urea compound given as intravenous injections over five to seven weeks; the standard treatment for stage-1 rhodesiense HAT and an alternative for stage-1 gambiense when pentamidine is unavailable. Requires a test dose first to check for adverse reactions. Cannot penetrate the CNS and is ineffective for stage-2 disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates above 95% for stage-1 rhodesiense when completed in full; ineffective for stage-2 CNS disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Pentamidine (intramuscular)
medicalTreatments.description: An aromatic diamidine given as intramuscular injections daily for seven days; first-line treatment for stage-1 gambiense HAT where fexinidazole is not yet available. Well-tolerated relative to older agents; does not cross the blood-brain barrier in therapeutic concentrations.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates of approximately 93-97% for stage-1 gambiense HAT in programme settings; ineffective for stage-2 disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Melarsoprol (intravenous)
medicalTreatments.description: An organoarsenic compound given intravenously; the only drug with consistent activity against CNS-stage rhodesiense HAT and a fallback for stage-2 gambiense where other options fail. Administered in a compressed 10-day schedule. Has the highest toxicity of all HAT treatments: post-treatment reactive encephalopathy (PTRE) occurs in 5-10% of recipients with 1-5% case fatality from the drug itself, plus peripheral neuropathy, cardiac toxicity, and renal impairment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates of 85-95% for rhodesiense stage-2 disease, but with significant treatment-related mortality that is ethically justified only given otherwise uniform fatality without treatment.

alternativeTreatments.name: Symptomatic management of neuropsychiatric symptoms
alternativeTreatments.description: During and after specific anti-trypanosomal treatment, sedatives, anticonvulsants, and nutritional support address seizures, insomnia, and wasting. These are supportive measures, not alternatives to parasitological treatment. No herbal or traditional remedy has been shown to clear trypanosomes.

alternativeTreatments.name: Vector control — tsetse fly trapping and targets
alternativeTreatments.description: Deployment of tsetse fly traps impregnated with attractants and insecticide, and insecticide-treated cattle (a reservoir-host management strategy for rhodesiense), have reduced transmission in several programmes. These are population-level public health measures rather than individual treatments, but they reduce the probability of re-infection after cure.

whySeeSpecialist: African trypanosomiasis must be managed by a tropical medicine specialist or infectious disease physician experienced with HAT. Treatment requires drug sourcing through WHO and national programmes, accurate disease staging with lumbar puncture, and follow-up CSF examination at 6, 12, and 24 months. For travellers presenting in non-endemic countries, specialist tropical medicine centres have access to the necessary diagnostic tests, WHO-supplied drugs, and expertise to avoid treatment errors that carry life-threatening consequences.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a tropical medicine specialist or infectious disease physician at a centre with experience in HAT diagnosis and WHO-coordinated drug access. In endemic countries, WHO-supported national sleeping sickness control programmes provide coordinated screening, diagnosis, treatment, and follow-up. For travellers presenting outside Africa, contact a specialist tropical medicine or imported infections unit — available in most major academic medical centres in Europe, North America, and Australia — who can access drugs through WHO and emergency importation channels.
preventionStrategies: Wear protective clothing in tsetse habitat: long sleeves, long trousers, and neutral or khaki colours (tsetse are attracted to dark and bright colours); apply DEET-containing insect repellent to exposed skin.
Use insecticide-treated screens and bed nets when staying in areas of active tsetse activity, even during daytime, as tsetse bite throughout the day.
Participate in WHO-coordinated active community screening programmes in endemic areas, which detect gambiense infection before CNS stage when treatment is safest and most effective.
Deploy tsetse fly traps and targets around villages and livestock compounds in endemic areas as part of integrated vector management, which has been central to recent case reductions.
Avoid bush clearance near inhabited areas during peak tsetse seasons without appropriate protection.
lifestyleModifications: Avoid entering dense riverine bush or game forest habitat in endemic areas without full protective clothing and insect repellent, particularly during daytime when tsetse flies feed.
Use insecticide-treated nets and screens even in daytime rest situations in field-based work in endemic zones.
Report fever, headache, or posterior neck swelling to a health worker promptly if you live in or have recently visited an endemic area — early-stage detection is curative with minimal toxicity.
Complete the full prescribed anti-trypanosomal treatment course and attend all follow-up CSF appointments, since premature stopping or missed follow-ups can miss treatment failure.
After recovery from HAT, individuals in endemic areas should continue to participate in active surveillance rounds to detect any re-infection.

recommended: High-protein, high-calorie diet during and after treatment to address the severe wasting and metabolic depletion that accumulate over months of HAT illness
Small frequent meals when nausea limits intake during fexinidazole or NECT treatment — fexinidazole must be taken with food to maximise absorption and tolerability
Oral rehydration solutions and electrolyte-rich fluids during febrile phase to compensate for fluid losses
Micronutrient-rich foods or supplementation during recovery given the generalised nutritional deficiency common in advanced HAT
avoid: Alcohol during fexinidazole treatment; fexinidazole is a nitroimidazole and concurrent alcohol use can provoke a disulfiram-like reaction with nausea and vomiting
Prolonged fasting before taking fexinidazole, since food is required for adequate drug absorption and to reduce gastrointestinal side effects
Self-treatment with unverified herbal preparations, which may delay life-saving specific anti-parasitic treatment

exerciseGuidelines: During active HAT infection, rest is appropriate; the systemic febrile illness, neurological symptoms, and medication side effects make vigorous exercise unsafe and counterproductive. After successful treatment and during the two-year follow-up period, gradual resumption of normal physical activity is encouraged as strength and neurological function recover. Patients treated at stage 2 may have lasting fatigue and cognitive symptoms for months as neuroinflammation resolves; activity should be tailored to individual tolerance.
dailyManagement: Take fexinidazole every day for the full 10-day course with a substantial meal; do not skip doses as this risks treatment failure.
Attend all scheduled follow-up clinic visits for CSF examination at months 6, 12, and 24 after treatment, since late relapse is detectable only by CSF analysis.
Report any new neurological symptoms — confusion, seizure, behaviour change, worsening sleep — after treatment immediately, as these may indicate relapse or post-treatment reactive encephalopathy.
Continue using insect repellent and protective clothing in endemic areas after recovery to prevent re-infection, as prior infection confers no lasting protective immunity.
Ensure national HAT control programme registration after diagnosis to enable epidemiological surveillance and case contact investigation.
prognosis: With timely treatment, prognosis for HAT is excellent at stage 1: cure rates exceed 93-97% with pentamidine (gambiense) or suramin (rhodesiense) and above 91% with fexinidazole. Stage-2 gambiense HAT treated with NECT achieves approximately 94% cure with low treatment mortality; fexinidazole achieves similar efficacy with oral convenience. Stage-2 rhodesiense HAT treated with melarsoprol has a cure rate of 85-95% but 1-5% treatment mortality from PTRE — a risk that is nonetheless far lower than the near-100% mortality of untreated CNS-stage disease. Long-term neurological sequelae, including cognitive impairment, personality change, and residual sleep disruption, occur in a proportion of patients treated at late CNS stage, particularly after melarsoprol; outcomes are better when treatment begins before severe CNS involvement. Without any treatment, both forms are uniformly fatal.
recoveryTimeline: Stage-1 gambiense patients treated with fexinidazole or pentamidine typically become aparasitaemic within days; systemic symptoms (fever, lymphadenopathy) resolve over one to two weeks. Stage-2 patients show slower neurological recovery — sleep patterns may take weeks to months to normalise, and cognitive symptoms can persist for several months as CNS inflammation resolves. Follow-up CSF examination is required at 6, 12, and 24 months; treatment is considered successful if CSF remains normal at 24 months with no clinical relapse. Rhodesiense patients treated with melarsoprol may require four to six weeks for full clinical stabilisation.
complications: CNS invasion and meningoencephalitis: failure to diagnose before stage 2 leads to irreversible neurological damage in some patients; severe cases result in permanent cognitive impairment, personality change, and epilepsy even after parasitological cure.

Post-treatment reactive encephalopathy (PTRE): a life-threatening inflammatory reaction occurring in 5-10% of melarsoprol recipients, characterised by seizures, coma, and rapid neurological deterioration; case fatality is 1-5%.

Pregnancy complications: HAT in pregnancy increases risk of maternal mortality, miscarriage, stillbirth, and neonatal infection via transplacental transmission; fexinidazole is contraindicated in pregnancy, limiting treatment options to NECT under specialist supervision.

Cardiac involvement in rhodesiense infection: trypanosomal myocarditis and pericarditis can cause arrhythmia and cardiac failure in the acute form; electrocardiographic monitoring is recommended during treatment.

Relapse: approximately 5-15% of treated patients relapse within 24 months, detectable only by follow-up CSF analysis; relapse after NECT or fexinidazole is managed with repeat treatment or acoziborole where available.

supportResources.name: WHO — Human African Trypanosomiasis
supportResources.description: WHO fact sheet covering epidemiology, control targets, diagnosis, and treatment with current global case data.

supportResources.name: CDC — African Trypanosomiasis (Sleeping Sickness)
supportResources.description: CDC patient and clinician resource covering transmission, diagnosis, and treatment for travellers and healthcare providers in non-endemic settings.

supportResources.name: Drugs for Neglected Diseases initiative (DNDi) — HAT
supportResources.description: Information on fexinidazole and acoziborole development, including access programmes and clinical trial data from the nonprofit that developed these treatments.

confusedWithConditions.name: Malaria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Malaria causes fever with strict periodicity (every 48 or 72 hours) and positive blood film for Plasmodium; HAT causes irregular fever with posterior cervical lymphadenopathy and a positive trypanosomes blood film or serology.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tuberculosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: TB typically causes productive cough, night sweats, and weight loss with pulmonary infiltrates on chest X-ray; HAT causes sleep cycle disruption, neuropsychiatric changes, and cervical lymphadenopathy with positive parasitology or serology specific to T. brucei.

confusedWithConditions.name: HIV and AIDS
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: HIV causes diffuse lymphadenopathy and systemic immunosuppression; HAT causes posterior cervical lymphadenopathy specifically (Winterbottom's sign) and is confirmed by trypanosome-specific serology and blood microscopy rather than HIV serology.

confusedWithConditions.name: Typhoid fever
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Typhoid presents with stepwise ascending fever, rose spots, and a positive Salmonella typhi culture or serology; HAT is distinguished by posterior cervical lymphadenopathy, tsetse exposure history, and trypanosome-specific testing.

relatedConditions.name: Chagas disease (American trypanosomiasis)
relatedConditions.relevance: Also caused by a Trypanosoma species (T. cruzi) transmitted by a blood-sucking insect vector, but found in the Americas and causing cardiac and gastrointestinal pathology; comparison illustrates the diversity of the genus.

relatedConditions.name: Leishmaniasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another WHO-classified neglected tropical disease caused by protozoa transmitted by insect vectors in tropical regions; visceral leishmaniasis shares the fever, wasting, and splenomegaly presentation and both fall under tropical medicine.

relatedConditions.name: Malaria
relatedConditions.relevance: The most important differential diagnosis of HAT in sub-Saharan Africa; both are vector-borne, febrile parasitic diseases co-endemic in much of the same geography, and co-infection occurs.

relatedConditions.name: Typhoid fever
relatedConditions.relevance: Enteric fever is another fever-with-systemic-illness diagnosis that clinicians must exclude before settling on HAT; both require specific laboratory tests for definitive differentiation.

faqs.question: What is sleeping sickness and why is it called that?
faqs.answer: Sleeping sickness is the common name for African trypanosomiasis, a parasitic infection caused by Trypanosoma brucei transmitted by tsetse flies. The name comes from the hallmark neurological symptom of stage-2 disease: severe disruption of the sleep-wake cycle, with extreme daytime sleepiness and inability to sleep at night, caused by parasites invading the brain and disrupting circadian regulation.

faqs.question: Is sleeping sickness still common?
faqs.answer: Cases have fallen dramatically. WHO reported fewer than 1,000 new cases per year by 2021, down from an estimated 300,000 per year in the late 1990s. This reduction resulted from WHO-coordinated active case-finding programmes, improved diagnostics, and safer treatments. Elimination as a public health problem is targeted for 2030, though the disease remains a risk in rural areas of 36 sub-Saharan African countries.

faqs.question: How is African trypanosomiasis transmitted?
faqs.answer: The disease is transmitted exclusively through the bite of infected tsetse flies (Glossina species) found in sub-Saharan Africa. The fly injects Trypanosoma brucei parasites into the skin when it bites. You cannot catch it from another person through normal contact; rare congenital transmission from mother to fetus and blood transfusion cases have been reported but are uncommon.

faqs.question: What are the two forms of African sleeping sickness?
faqs.answer: T. b. gambiense causes a slow chronic form (over 95% of cases) found in West and Central Africa that may smoulder for months to years before CNS symptoms appear. T. b. rhodesiense causes a rapid, acute form in East Africa that progresses to fatal CNS disease within weeks. Gambiense is primarily a human reservoir disease; rhodesiense is a zoonosis with an animal reservoir.

faqs.question: What does sleeping sickness do to the brain?
faqs.answer: In stage 2, trypanosomes cross the blood-brain barrier and invade brain tissue, triggering chronic meningoencephalitis. This disrupts the circadian pacemaker in the hypothalamus, causing profound inversion of the sleep-wake cycle — daytime somnolence and nighttime wakefulness. Continued CNS inflammation causes progressive confusion, motor disturbances, personality change, seizures, and ultimately coma if untreated.

faqs.question: Can sleeping sickness be cured?
faqs.answer: Yes, sleeping sickness is curable if treated. For T. b. gambiense, oral fexinidazole achieves cure rates above 91% for both early and non-severe late-stage disease. Stage-1 disease has cure rates of 93-97% with pentamidine or suramin. Without treatment, both forms are uniformly fatal.

faqs.question: What is the new oral treatment for sleeping sickness?
faqs.answer: Fexinidazole is a 10-day oral nitroimidazole approved by the European Medicines Agency in 2018 and recommended by WHO as first-line for T. b. gambiense HAT at both stage 1 and non-severe stage 2. It replaced the need for intravenous treatment in most gambiense cases, improving access and tolerability. A single-dose oral drug called acoziborole showed over 95% efficacy in trials and is undergoing regulatory review.

faqs.question: How is African trypanosomiasis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires demonstration of the parasite. For T. b. gambiense, a rapid card agglutination test (CATT or RDT) screens blood, then microscopy of blood or lymph node aspirate confirms parasites. Staging requires a lumbar puncture for CSF analysis. T. b. rhodesiense has no validated serological test and relies solely on blood microscopy during febrile illness investigation.

faqs.question: Is there a vaccine for sleeping sickness?
faqs.answer: No vaccine exists for African trypanosomiasis. The parasite evades vaccine development by continuously switching its outer protein coat (variant surface glycoprotein), generating thousands of antigenically distinct variants that overwhelm the immune system. Prevention relies on insect avoidance, vector control (tsetse traps and targets), and early detection through active community screening.

faqs.question: Who is most at risk of sleeping sickness?
faqs.answer: People most at risk are rural subsistence farmers, fishermen, and hunters in tsetse-endemic areas of sub-Saharan Africa — particularly the Democratic Republic of Congo, which accounts for over 75% of global gambiense cases. Wildlife tourists visiting East African national parks face low but real risk of rhodesiense infection. Travellers in remote endemic areas and humanitarian workers are also at risk.

faqs.question: How can I prevent sleeping sickness while travelling in Africa?
faqs.answer: Wear long-sleeved shirts and trousers in neutral or khaki colours — tsetse flies are attracted to dark and bright colours. Apply DEET insect repellent to exposed skin. Tsetse flies bite during the day, so protection is needed throughout daylight hours in woodland and riverine areas. There is no preventive drug or vaccine, so avoidance of tsetse habitat is the main strategy.

faqs.question: What is Winterbottom's sign and why does it matter?
faqs.answer: Winterbottom's sign is the swelling of posterior cervical lymph nodes — glands at the back of the neck — that occurs in up to 75% of T. b. gambiense infections during stage 1. It is an important clinical clue because most causes of lymphadenopathy affect anterior or multiple node groups; posterior cervical lymphadenopathy in someone from an endemic area should prompt HAT testing.

faqs.question: What is the difference between African and American trypanosomiasis?
faqs.answer: African trypanosomiasis (sleeping sickness) is caused by T. brucei, transmitted by tsetse flies in sub-Saharan Africa, and primarily affects the nervous system with sleep disruption. American trypanosomiasis (Chagas disease) is caused by T. cruzi, transmitted by triatomine bugs in Latin America, and primarily affects the heart and gastrointestinal tract with lifelong chronic disease. Both are neglected tropical diseases caused by protozoa.

faqs.question: Why is lumbar puncture required before treating sleeping sickness?
faqs.answer: The choice of treatment depends entirely on disease stage. Stage-1 treatments (pentamidine, suramin) do not penetrate the CNS and would leave parasites in the brain untreated. Stage-2 treatments (NECT, fexinidazole for CNS cases, melarsoprol) are needed when CSF shows parasites or elevated white cells. Treating with the wrong drug leads to relapse or inadequate CNS clearance, so accurate staging is mandatory.

faqs.question: Can sleeping sickness be caught more than once?
faqs.answer: Yes. Prior infection with T. brucei does not confer protective immunity. The parasite's antigenic variation means the immune system cannot remember a defence that is effective against future infections. People in endemic areas can be reinfected after successful treatment. This is why continued vector-control and surveillance measures are important even for treated individuals.

faqs.question: How long does it take sleeping sickness to become fatal without treatment?
faqs.answer: T. b. rhodesiense progresses to fatal CNS disease within weeks to a few months without treatment, due to its more virulent biology. T. b. gambiense has a much slower course — many people remain in stage 1 for years before CNS involvement, but the disease is ultimately fatal in virtually all untreated cases. The chronic timeline of gambiense is also why mass screening is so critical for early detection.

faqs.question: Is sleeping sickness a concern for safari tourists?
faqs.answer: The risk for tourists is low but real, particularly for T. b. rhodesiense in East African game parks (Uganda, Tanzania, Zambia). Imported rhodesiense cases in travellers are periodically reported. Tourists should use insect repellent and protective clothing in woodland and bush habitat. Any fever developing within weeks of returning from East Africa should prompt investigation that includes HAT testing alongside malaria.

faqs.question: What causes the sleep disruption in sleeping sickness?
faqs.answer: Trypanosomes invading the CNS during stage 2 disrupt the suprachiasmatic nucleus and other hypothalamic structures that regulate the circadian sleep-wake cycle. They also alter levels of melatonin, prostaglandin D2, and other sleep-regulating molecules. The result is fragmented sleep scattered throughout the 24-hour cycle, with pronounced somnolence during daytime — the symptom that names the disease.

faqs.question: What happens if sleeping sickness is treated in stage 1 versus stage 2?
faqs.answer: Treatment at stage 1 carries cure rates above 93-97% with minimal toxicity using pentamidine or suramin; neurological damage has not yet occurred. Stage-2 treatment with NECT achieves around 94% cure but requires intravenous drugs; melarsoprol for rhodesiense carries up to 5% drug-related fatality. Some patients treated at late CNS stage retain neurological sequelae including cognitive impairment even after parasitological cure.

faqs.question: What follow-up is needed after sleeping sickness treatment?
faqs.answer: After treatment, patients require lumbar puncture and CSF examination at 6, 12, and 24 months to confirm cure. Relapse is detectable only by CSF changes — a returning white cell count or reappearance of parasites — and may occur without obvious clinical symptoms. WHO national HAT programmes typically manage this follow-up in endemic countries; travellers treated abroad should attend a specialist tropical medicine centre.

faqs.question: Can sleeping sickness be spread by blood transfusion?
faqs.answer: Transmission via blood transfusion and shared needles has been documented but is rare compared with tsetse-borne transmission. In endemic countries with limited blood screening capacity, transfusion-transmitted HAT is a recognised though uncommon risk. Donors who have lived in or travelled through endemic areas are deferred from blood donation in many high-income countries.

metaDescription: African trypanosomiasis (sleeping sickness) is a tsetse fly-borne parasite causing sleep disruption and brain damage. Learn symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment.
